DashboardNext Lesson

Managing Effects – Part 2

react.gg
Managing Effects – Part 2

In the previous lesson, we established three rules for managing effects in React. They were...

Rule #0

When a component renders, it should do so without running into any side effects

Rule #1

If a side effect is triggered by an event, put that side effect in an event handler

Rule #2

If a side effect is synchronizing your component with some outside system, put that side effect inside useEffect

Now that you have some rules to guide you, let's stress test them a bit with some more advanced use cases.

Up until this point, all of our examples have been side effects that are local to the user's device – getting their battery level, accessing local storage, changing the document's title. But when you think of side effects, especially as someone building for the web, the one that most likely comes to mind first is network requests.

Without the ability to manage the complexity of asynchronous side effects like network requests, React would be mostly useless. Thankfully, we've already established a nice mental model for how we can do this.

To demonstrate it, let's say we were building a carousel like experience whose content was different Pokémon from the PokéAPI.

Now before we jump into the code, I want you, knowing what you know now about how to handle side effects in React, to think through how you might implement this.

We know based on that we need to keep our network request out of React's rendering flow. Now we just need to decide where we put it, either in an event handler or in useEffect.

Your first intuition is probably to put it in an event handler. Whenever the user interacts with our carousel, we'll make a network request to asynchronously fetch the next Pokémon. That seems perfectly reasonable – unfortunately, it's the wrong approach.

This becomes obvious when we break it down further. What exactly does "whenever the user interacts with our carousel" actually mean? When they click a button? What about navigating with their keyboard? What if we wanted our carousel to change Pokemon automatically every few seconds? What about on the initial render when there's no user interaction but we still need to fetch the first Pokémon?

The take away here is there's no single event that should trigger our network request. Instead, what we're really wanting to do is synchronize our component with the result of calling the Pokémon API – the perfect use case for useEffect.

Before we worry the async bits, let's build out the basic logic for our carousel first. Again, the entire premise of this app is to synchronize our component with the result of calling the Pokémon API. To do that, we'll need some state. Specifically, we'll add an id state to keep track of the id of the Pokémon we're currently viewing and a pokemon state to keep track of the actual Pokémon data. Our id state will default to 1 since that's the id of the first Pokémon, Bulbasaur.

import * as React from "react"

export default function App () {
  const [id, setId] = React.useState(1)
  const [pokemon, setPokemon] = React.useState(null)

  return (
    <main>
      {JSON.stringify({ id, pokemon }, null, 2)}
    </main>
  )
}

We'll then encapsulate the async logic for fetching a Pokémon into a function called fetchPokemon. Now, whenever we invoke fetchPokemon passing it an id of the Pokémon we want to fetch, we'll get back an object with an error if there was one or a response of the Pokémon data if there wasn't.

export async function fetchPokemon (id) {
  try {
    const res = await fetch(
      `https://pokeapi.co/api/v2/pokemon/${id}`
    )

    if (res.ok === true) {
      return {
        error: null,
        response: await res.json()
      }
    }

    throw new Error(`Error fetching pokemon #${id}`)
  } catch (e) {
    return {
      error: e,
      response: null
    }
  }
}

Now the fun part. We want to synchronize our local pokemon state with the PokéAPI according to our local id state. That's a fancy way of saying whenever our local id state changes, we want to invoke fetchPokemon passing it the new id. We'll do that by calling fetchPokemon inside of useEffect and then updating our pokemon state with the result.

import * as React from "react"
import { fetchPokemon } from "./api"

export default function App () {
  const [id, setId] = React.useState(1)
  const [pokemon, setPokemon] = React.useState(null)

  React.useEffect(() => {
    const handleFetchPokemon = async () => {
      const { error, response } = await fetchPokemon(id)
      setPokemon(response)
    }

    handleFetchPokemon()
  }, [id])

  return (
    <main>
      {JSON.stringify({ id, pokemon }, null, 2)}
    </main>
  )
}

There are a few interesting things to point out with the code above.

First, notice that our dependency array only includes one element, id. Again, this isn't telling React to re-run the effect when id changes (though, that's what happens). Instead, we're giving React an array of all of the dependencies our effect needs to re-synchronize with the outside system. As a byproduct of that, whenever React sees that id has changed between renders, it will re-run the effect.

Second, you may be wondering why we declared a new handleFetchPokemon function inside of our event. This is purely aesthetic and was done so that we could use async/await inside of useEffect.

Third, this is "tutorial" code. We're pretending like loading and error states don't exist. Unfortunately, you can't write tutorial code at work, so let's fix that.

First, we'll need to add two new pieces of state to our component, loading and error. Because fetchPokemon is invoked as a result of rendering, including the initial render, we'll initialize loading to true (and error to null).

const [loading, setLoading] = React.useState(true)
const [error, setError] = React.useState(null)

Now we need our effect to manage our new loading and error states. Thankfully our fetchPokemon function makes this easy since it's so explicit if there's an error.

What we'll do is whenever our effects runs, reset loading and error back to their default values before we make the network request. Then, once we get back a response, if there's an error, we'll update error, and if not, we'll update pokemon before setting loading to false.

import * as React from "react"
import { fetchPokemon } from "./api"

export default function App () {
  const [id, setId] = React.useState(1)
  const [pokemon, setPokemon] = React.useState(null)
  const [loading, setLoading] = React.useState(true)
  const [error, setError] = React.useState(null)

  React.useEffect(() => {
    const handleFetchPokemon = async () => {
      setLoading(true)
      setError(null)

      const { error, response } = await fetchPokemon(id)

      if (error) {
        setError(error.message)
      } else {
        setPokemon(response)
      }

      setLoading(false)
    }

    handleFetchPokemon()
  }, [id])

  return (
    <main>
      {JSON.stringify({ id, loading, error, pokemon }, null, 2)}
    </main>
  )
}
🆗

State Batching

Don't get freaked out over the multiple, separate invocations of our updater functions (setError, setPokemon, setLoading). Remember, React "batches" those together intelligently so there's no wasted renders.

You could argue at this point the hardest parts are behind us. It appears we've successfully abstracted the arduous async side effect bits into useEffect, and are now synchronizing our component with the PokéAPI.

At this point, all we need to do is update the UI to both show our active Pokémon and allow the user (or the app itself) to change it via our Carousel. These components are extraneous to the point of this post, so I'll just paste them in and you can view them if you'd like.

import * as React from "react"
import { fetchPokemon } from "./api"
import Carousel from "./Carousel"
import PokemonCard from "./PokemonCard"

export default function App () {
  const [id, setId] = React.useState(1)
  const [pokemon, setPokemon] = React.useState(null)
  const [loading, setLoading] = React.useState(true)
  const [error, setError] = React.useState(null)

  const handlePrevious = () => {
    if (id > 1) {
      setId(id - 1) 
    }
  }

  const handleNext = () => setId(id + 1)

  React.useEffect(() => {
    const handleFetchPokemon = async () => {
      setLoading(true)
      setError(null)

      const { error, response } = await fetchPokemon(id)

      if (error) {
        setError(error.message)
      } else {
        setPokemon(response)
      }

      setLoading(false)
    }

    handleFetchPokemon()
  }, [id])

  return (
    <Carousel onPrevious={handlePrevious} onNext={handleNext}>
      <PokemonCard 
        loading={loading} 
        error={error} 
        data={pokemon} 
      />
    </Carousel>
  )
}

Notice the simplicity of our app now. Because we've told useEffect to synchronize our local pokemon state with the PokéAPI according to id, we've taken what has historically been the most complex part of building a web app, an asynchronous side effect, and made it an implementation detail behind simply updating id via our handlePrevious and handleNext functions.

Now, unfortunately, we're not quite done yet. As is, our code contains the worst kind of bug – one that is both inconspicuous and deceptively wasteful. Can you spot it?

If not,

Whenever we call fetchPokemon, because it's an asynchronous request, we have no idea how long that request will take to resolve. It's completely possible that, while we're in the process of waiting for a response, our Carousel updates id, which causes a re-render, which causes our effect to run again with a different id.

In this scenario, we now have two requests in flight, both with different ids. Worse, we have no way of knowing which one will resolve first. In both scenarios, we're calling setPokemon when the request resolves. That means, because we don't know in which order they'll resolve, pokemon, and therefor our UI, will eventually be whatever request was resolved last. To make it worse, you'll also get a flash of the Pokémon that resolves first, before the second one does.

You can see this by scrolling back up and playing around with the app. Change the active Pokémon on the carousel as fast as you can.

That's a pretty subpar experience. How would you go about fixing it?

Really what we want to do is to tell React to ignore any responses that come from requests that were made in effects that are no longer relevant. In order to do that, of course, we need a way to know if an effect is the latest one. If not, then we should ignore the response and not setPokemon inside of it.

Thankfully, React makes this pretty easy with the last remaining feature of useEffect you haven't seen yet: the "cleanup" function.

If you return a function from your effect, React will call that function each time before it ever calls your effect again, and then one final time when the component is removed from the DOM.

import * as React from "react"

export default function App () {
  const [count, setCount] = React.useState(0)

  React.useEffect(() => {
    console.log(`In effect: ${count}`)
    return () => {
      console.log(`In cleanup: ${count}`)
    }
  }, [count])

  const handleClick = () => setCount(count + 1)

  if (count > 3) {
    return null
  }

  return (
    <button onClick={handleClick}>
      {count}
    </button>
  )
}

We can see this in action by adding a cleanup function to our effect that logs the id that the effect is associated with.

Now play around with the app and notice the logs. Specifically, think of how we can leverage this knowledge of our cleanup function in order to ignore stale effects.

import * as React from "react"
import { fetchPokemon } from "./api"
import Carousel from "./Carousel"
import PokemonCard from "./PokemonCard"

export default function App () {
  const [id, setId] = React.useState(1)
  const [pokemon, setPokemon] = React.useState(null)
  const [loading, setLoading] = React.useState(true)
  const [error, setError] = React.useState(null)

  const handlePrevious = () => {
    if (id > 1) {
      setId(id - 1) 
    }
  }

  const handleNext = () => setId(id + 1)

  React.useEffect(() => {
    const handleFetchPokemon = async () => {
      setLoading(true)
      setError(null)

      const { error, response } = await fetchPokemon(id)

      if (error) {
        setError(error.message)
      } else {
        setPokemon(response)
      }

      setLoading(false)
    }

    handleFetchPokemon()

    return () => {
      console.log(`The cleanup function for ${id} was called`)
    }
  }, [id])

  return (
    <Carousel onPrevious={handlePrevious} onNext={handleNext}>
      <PokemonCard 
        loading={loading} 
        error={error} 
        data={pokemon} 
      />
    </Carousel>
  )
}

Notice that the cleanup function is only called for ids that are no longer relevant. This makes sense because the cleanup function for the most recent effect won't be called until either another effect runs (making it stale) or the component has been removed from the DOM (irrelevant in this scenario).

We can use this knowledge along with the Power of JavaScript™ to fix our problem.

Whenever the effect runs, let's make a variable called ignore and set it to false. Then, whenever the cleanup function runs (which we know will only happen when the effect is stale), we'll set ignore to true.

Then, all we have to do before we call setPokemon or setError, is check to see if ignore is true. If it is, then we'll do nothing.

import * as React from "react"
import { fetchPokemon } from "./api"
import Carousel from "./Carousel"
import PokemonCard from "./PokemonCard"

export default function App () {
  const [id, setId] = React.useState(1)
  const [pokemon, setPokemon] = React.useState(null)
  const [loading, setLoading] = React.useState(true)
  const [error, setError] = React.useState(null)

  const handlePrevious = () => {
    if (id > 1) {
      setId(id - 1) 
    }
  }

  const handleNext = () => setId(id + 1)

  React.useEffect(() => {
    let ignore = false

    const handleFetchPokemon = async () => {
      setLoading(true)
      setError(null)

      const { error, response } = await fetchPokemon(id)

      if (ignore) {
        return
      } else if (error) {
        setError(error.message)
      } else {
        setPokemon(response)
      }

      setLoading(false)
    }

    handleFetchPokemon()

    return () => {
      ignore = true
    }
  }, [id])

  return (
    <Carousel onPrevious={handlePrevious} onNext={handleNext}>
      <PokemonCard 
        loading={loading} 
        error={error} 
        data={pokemon} 
      />
    </Carousel>
  )
}

Now, regardless of how many times id changes, we'll ignore every response that isn't in the most recent effect. This not only makes our app more performant, but it also improves the UX since React will now only re-render with the latest Pokémon.

Now I know you're having the time of your life, but this'll be the last scenario we look at for now – and it's one you'll see often.

Let's say we were writing software for a company that administers certifications for employees. To earn a certification, an employee must pass an online test by a certain percentage. Part of the test is that for every time the employee tabs away from the current window, they lose a percentage.

How would we go about encapsulating this requirement into a React component, letting the student know how many times they've tabbed away?

🎁

Here's a hint

You can use the document's visibilitychange event to detect when the page is no longer visible.

First, let's add some state to track the number of times the employee has tabbed away. We'll call this count and we'll also add some UI for the user to see their count.

import * as React from "react"

export default function TabAways () {
  const [count, setCount] = React.useState(0)

  return (
    <p>
      You've tabbed away <strong>{count}</strong> time{count !== 1 && "s"}.
    </p>
  )
}

Now the tricky part. We need to listen for the visibilitychange event and update our count state accordingly. Since we're synchronizing the document's visibility status with our component, we'll use useEffect.

First let's add our event listener. Remember, whenever you add an event listener, you also need to remove it when the component is removed from the DOM or you'll have a memory leak. useEffect's cleanup function makes this simple.

import * as React from "react"

export default function TabAways () {
  const [count, setCount] = React.useState(0)
  
  React.useEffect(() => {
    const handleChange = () => {

    }

    document.addEventListener("visibilitychange", handleChange)

    return () => {
      document.removeEventListener("visibilitychange", handleChange)
    }
  }, [])

  return (
    <p>
      You've tabbed away <strong>{count}</strong> time{count !== 1 && "s"}.
    </p>
  )
}

Now all we need to do is update our handleChange function to increment our count state whenever the document is no longer visible. Your first intuition might be to do something like this – where we add count to our dependency array and then increment it by 1 inside of handleChange.

import * as React from "react"

export default function TabAways () {
  const [count, setCount] = React.useState(0)
  
  React.useEffect(() => {
    const handleChange = () => {
      if (document.visibilityState !== "visible") {
        setCount(count + 1)
      }
    }

    document.addEventListener("visibilitychange", handleChange)

    return () => {
      document.removeEventListener("visibilitychange", handleChange)
    }
  }, [count])

  return (
    <p>
      You've tabbed away <strong>{count}</strong> time{count !== 1 && "s"}.
    </p>
  )
}

Whenever the document's visibilityState is no longer visible, we increment count by one. Simple, right? Yes, but actually no. In fact, even though our app appears to be working fine, there's a major problem. Can you spot it?

Via our dependency array, we've told React that our effect depends on our count state (which is true). That means, whenever count changes, React will re-render and then, once it's done, re-invoke our effect.

The problem is every time React calls our effect, we're removing the previous event listener and adding a new one. That's... not ideal. We can see this in action if we add some logs to our effect and tab away and back to this page.

import * as React from "react"

export default function TabAways () {
  const [count, setCount] = React.useState(0)
  
  React.useEffect(() => {
    const handleChange = () => {
      if (document.visibilityState !== "visible") {
        setCount(count + 1)
      }
    }

    console.log("addEventListener")
    document.addEventListener("visibilitychange", handleChange)

    return () => {
      console.log("removeEventListener")
      document.removeEventListener("visibilitychange", handleChange)
    }
  }, [count])

  return (
    <p>
      You've tabbed away <strong>{count}</strong> time{count !== 1 && "s"}.
    </p>
  )
}

We're in a weird spot. We only want React to add our event listener once, on the initial mount, but we need to include count in the dependency array because we're using it inside of the effect to calculate the new count state.

What's interesting about this example compared to our other examples is, even though we're using count in the effect, we're not using it as a way to synchronize our component with some external system – so we don't actually need React re-run the effect when it changes.

Thankfully, React has a solution for these types of scenarios and it's probably not what you'd expect. In fact, it has nothing to do with useEffect at all. Instead, it has to do with how we're updating our count state.

Right now, and in almost every other scenario when you're updating state, you pass the state's updater function the value with which you want to replace the current state.

const handleChange = () => {
  if (document.visibilityState !== "visible") {
    setCount(count + 1)
  }
}

However, an often overlooked aspect of this API is you're also able to pass the state's updater function a function itself, and when React invokes that function, it'll pass it the current state. You can then use that state to calculate the new state.

const handleChange = () => {
  if (document.visibilityState !== "visible") {
    setCount((c) => c + 1)
  }
}

In both of these examples, we're telling React to increment count by 1. However, the difference is in how we're getting access to the current state of count before we increment it. In the first example, we're relying on a closure – and therefore we have to specify count inside of the dependency array. In the second, we're not, since React will pass us the current state of count as an argument and therefore, we don't need to include it in the dependency array.

With this knowledge, we can now update our example to remove count from the dependency array which prevents React from re-running our effect every time it changes.

import * as React from "react"

export default function TabAways () {
  const [count, setCount] = React.useState(0)
  
  React.useEffect(() => {
    const handleChange = () => {
      if (document.visibilityState !== "visible") {
        setCount((c) => c + 1)
      }
    }

    console.log("addEventListener")
    document.addEventListener("visibilitychange", handleChange)

    return () => {
      console.log("removeEventListener")
      document.removeEventListener("visibilitychange", handleChange)
    }
  }, [])

  return (
    <p>
      You've tabbed away <strong>{count}</strong> time{count !== 1 && "s"}.
    </p>
  )
}

Hopefully at this point you're starting to get a solid grasp of how you can use the rules we've established to better establish a mental model for managing side effects in React.

Now before you go, there are a few smaller details I want to point out that didn't fit into the normal narrative flow of these last few lessons, but I think are important to know.

StrictMode

First is how StrictMode affects useEffect. If you're not familiar, StrictMode is a component that comes with React that allows you to stress test your application in development.

import * as React from "react"
import { createRoot } from "react-dom/client"
import App from "./App.jsx"


const root = createRoot(document.getElementById("root"))


root.render(
  <React.StrictMode>
    <App />
  </React.StrictMode>
)

When you have StrictMode "enabled" (which is the default for most React apps), React will re-render an extra time, re-run effects an extra time, and check for deprecated APIs.

Why is this helpful? You can think of StrictMode as a stress test of our 3 rules. If render is not pure or you're incorrectly managing side effects, StrictMode will (aggressively) uncover that.

It's controversial, but the whole goal of StrictMode is to make it more obvious that you're doing something wrong – which I think is a reasonable goal. If you stick to the rules we've established, StrictMode will be mostly irrelevant for you.

For more information on it, check out the official documentation.

"Reactive" Values

Throughout our discussion on effects, you may have been wondering the distinction between what needs to be included in useEffect's dependency array and what doesn't. For example, why doesn't localStorage, Number, or setIndex need to be included in our Greeting effect?

React.useEffect(() => {
  const item = localStorage.getItem("index")


  if (item) {
    setIndex(Number(item))
  }
}, [])

The reason is because they're not reactive values. Defined, a reactive value is any value that can change between re-renders. Props, state, or any variables defined inside of a component are all reactive values – and therefore, always need to be included in the dependency array if they're used in the effect.

In our example, localStorage and Number are always going to reference the same spot in memory and React guarantees that the setIndex updater function will also always be consistent across renders, meaning all of these are non reactive values.

This makes sense if you think about it. The whole goal of useEffect is to synchronize your component with some outside system. Non reactive values are irrelevant to useEffect because they're never used in the synchronization process.

The other 5%

The rules we've established will work for 95% of the cases you'll encounter when dealing with side effects in React. As these last few lessons have already been long enough, we'll cover that other 5% in a later lesson. But for those curious, here's a full list of the rules I follow, including that 5%.

Rule #0

When a component renders, it should do so without running into any side effects

Rule #1

If a side effect is triggered by an event, put that side effect in an event handler

Rule #2

If a side effect is synchronizing your component with some outside system, put that side effect inside useEffect

Rule #3

If a side effect is synchronizing your component with some outside system and that side effect needs to run *before* the browser paints the screen, put that side effect inside useLayoutEffect

Rule #4

If a side effect is subscribing to an external store, use the useSyncExternalStore hook

5 months ago

Can someone explain the cleanup function for our Pokemon carousel?

If you return a function from your effect, React will call that function each time before it ever calls your effect again...

I see this after adding logs into the effect's return and handleFetchPokemon():

inside return and ignore is: true
inside handleFetchPokemon and ignore is: false

ignore is false when the effect is called because we set it to false at the top of the effect, let ignore = false.

But when I click through the carousel 5 times, ignore is only set to false on the most recent render, how??:

inside return and ignore is: true
inside return and ignore is: true
inside return and ignore is: true
inside return and ignore is: true
inside return and ignore is: true
inside handleFetchPokemon and ignore is: true
inside handleFetchPokemon and ignore is: true
inside handleFetchPokemon and ignore is: true
inside handleFetchPokemon and ignore is: true
inside handleFetchPokemon and ignore is: false

I know that's what we want to accomplish to prevent the interstitial renders when paging through the carousel, but, before the most recent render, how is ignore still true when it gets into handleFetchPokemon() when let ignore = false in defined earlier in the effect?

Ok, this one was huge.

this isn't telling React to re-run the effect when id changes (though, that's what happens). Instead, we're giving React an array of all of the dependencies our effect needs to re-synchronize with the outside system. As a byproduct of that, whenever React sees that id has changed between renders, it will re-run the effect.

I... don't understand what you were trying to convey here. Like, at all. :/ For me, it was a lot of mumbo-jumbo and then it just reaffirms the simple "change in id == rerun the render" conclusion.

you may be wondering why we declared a new handleFetchPokemon function inside of our event. This is purely aesthetic and was done so that we could use async/await inside of useEffect.

    const fresh = useRef();
    const [date, setDate] = useState("");

    useEffect(() => {
        // fetchOrders();
        // let fresh: any = true;        
        fresh.current = true;

        (async () => {
                        
            try {
                const res = await fetch("http://example.com");

                const { success, data, message } = res;

                if (!success) {
                    throw new Error(message);
                }

                // console.log(fresh.current);

                    console.log('skipped');
                }
            } catch (error) {
                // handle error
            }
        })();

        return () => {
            fresh.current = false;
            // fresh = false;

I'm still confused as to what the order of operations/steps things happen in is when one creates a stale effect in the Pokemon app by clicking the arrows a bunch of times quickly.

I know the cleanup function runs before the effect but I'm having a hard time seeing how ignore set to true is used when it's declared and set to false every time the effect runs.

Logs for reference:

Initial render of Pokemon app:

Effect ran! ignore is: [false]
setPokemon with id: [1]

Click forward arrow button twice fast:

Cleanup function called for id: [1]
Effect ran! ignore is: [false] id: [2]
Cleanup function called for id: [2]
Effect ran! ignore is: [false] id: [3]
ignore is: [true] for id: [2] - DO NOTHING
setPokemon with id: [3]

Juan Ospina
4 months ago

This was a great lesson and it really helped me to better understand effects and more specifically the useEffect hook. However i got a bit lost when you introduced the cleanup functions to fix the issue of too many request being made at the same time.

React.useEffect(() => {
    let ignore = false

    const handleFetchPokemon = async () => {
      setLoading(true)
      setError(null)

      const { error, response } = await fetchPokemon(id)

      if (ignore) {
        return
      } else if (error) {
        setError(error.message)
      } else {
        setPokemon(response)
      }

      setLoading(false)
    }

    handleFetchPokemon()

    return () => {
      ignore = true
    }
  }, [id])

Here you used an ignore variable that is declared inside of the effect. From the lesson i understood that the flow goes like this:

useEffect runs -> Cleans up when a new invocation of the useEffect is made -> useEffect runs again

So why does the ignore variable retains it's value after useEffect runs again?

Eric Nichols 2 months ago

I see several people were confused about the "ignore" variable, and Tyler clarified regarding the closures in useEffect etc.

Where I was confused was understanding when the "cleanup" function was called. I think it's getting called while we're still waiting on the async network request. So the control flow in the double-request scenario is like this:

  1. Sets ignore to false
  2. Start the async await
  3. Some other instance of useEffect runs, since the user clicked the button etc. (I'm not sure precisely when the next step runs, but it's perhaps just after this new useEffect finishes? Or maybe just before it runs?)
  4. Now as a result of 3 the cleanup function for that first instance of useEffect runs, setting its ignore to true
  5. Finally the async call finishes
  6. Now we check the ignore status and it's true so we're done.
Jubril Akinsanya 2 months ago

Thankfully, React has a solution for these types of scenarios and it's probably not what you'd expect. In fact, it has nothing to do with useEffect at all. Instead, it has to do with how we're updating our count state.

I was confused at this step, cause I think it was not clear enough to me that it was mandatory to put ALL THE DEPENDENCIES in the DepArr, I thought you could just put it when you wanted react to actually care about this dependencies changes.

So I did not understand why you put count in the DepArr, I thought you could just remove it and solve the problem.

I think for others, it would be cool to insist a little bit more in the "Managing Effects" lesson that adding all the dependencies in the DepArr is not an optionnal thing to do.

Jubril Akinsanya 2 months ago

Now the tricky part. We need to listen for the visibilitychange event and update our count state accordingly. Since we're synchronizing the document's visibility status with our component, we'll use useEffect.

This part as well is a bit confusing to me cause it sounds like the user getting out of the tab is both an Event and depends on an Outside System, so I was confused if we should use Rule #1 or Rule #2

I don't clearly see what is the line between both then.

If the code in that function was used in multiple places then I'd lean towards extracting it into a module, but now I'm questioning myself.

I ask as I have seen this done in multiple projects over the last few years.

Ganbin 56 days ago

I was hoping that you would explain why in that code :

React.useEffect(() => {
    const handleFetchPokemon = async () => {
      setLoading(true)
      setError(null)

      const { error, response } = await fetchPokemon(id)

      if (error) {
        setError(error.message)
      } else {
        setPokemon(response)
      }

      setLoading(false)
    }

    handleFetchPokemon()

    return () => {
      console.log(`The cleanup function for ${id} was called`)
    }
  }, [id])

Why the setLoading is not "batched" and only the latest call false will be used in the next re-render. I suppose this is because an await is called between the 2 calls.

Is that correct to say that if there is an await statement, the re-render will be done directly and not at the end of the current execution cycle (end of the function)?

Another question is:

Why, in the latest example, the code:

    const handleChange = () => {
      if (document.visibilityState !== "visible") {
        setCount(count + 1)
      }
    }

Is not declared outside of the useEffect in order to not have the count variable as a dependency?

Kirk Is 53 days ago

Yeah, it's interesting. Like, I feel "add a document listener" is the most basic use of useEffect's cleanup, the easiest to understand.

The pokemon "ignore" usage is trickier... i had forgotten about useEffect's cleanup "called if this is called again". So it's... weird. Like, the first load is put into play, then the button is clicked again so a second load happens, but that causes the first loads clean up to happen so it knows not to change the world. Oof, that's kinda complex! like the two handlers are sort of interleaved.

And of course, the "use setSomething((old)=>new)" paradigm which means you might be able to include a value you want to change as a dependency is even subtler.

Maybe another more basic "document handler" useEffect (with cleanup), THEN Pokemon "useEffect cleanup to let the last one win", THEN document handler use setState with a function instead of needing to depend on a reactive example? I dunno. I just wish it was more clear that complexity was building across the examples, that interleaved Pokemon handler is tricky to think about as the first thing...

Mukhammadjavokhirbek 34 days ago

In useEffect "cleanup" function we worked on ignore variable. In Carousel.tsx "cleanup" function removes the event listener from the window object. This "cleanup" function is required to prevent window having as many listeners as the state changed and to have only one working.

Also, in "tab away" example it was said the event listener removal helps prevent memory leak. So, in Carousel component "cleanup" function guarantees we have only one event listener working and the prevention of memory leak.

Did I get this right?

Mukhammadjavokhirbek 34 days ago

In both of these examples, we're telling React to increment count by 1. However, the difference is in how we're getting access to the current state of count before we increment it. In the first example, we're relying on a closure – and therefore we have to specify count inside of the dependency array. In the second, we're not, since React will pass us the current state of count as an argument and therefore, we don't need to include it in the dependency array.

I have gone through "The Ultimate Guide to Hoisting, Scopes, and Closures in JavaScript" one more time. But couldn't get the connection between relying on a closure and the updater function.
